title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Drawing the World: The Science and Art of Cartography
content.text: A map is never simply a picture of the world. Every map is a series of decisions — what to include, what to leave out, where to place the centre, how to represent a curved surface on a flat page. These choices are never neutral, and they reveal as much about the people who made them as about the territory they depict. (37) [37] Los mapas reflejan la visión del mundo de quienes los crean. [/37] The history of cartography is, in this sense, also a history of power, belief and the human desire to impose order on the unfamiliar. || The oldest surviving maps are clay tablets from ancient Mesopotamia, dating back around four thousand five hundred years. They show not the world as we know it but a local landscape — fields, rivers and property boundaries relevant to their owners. (38) [38] Los primeros mapas eran documentos prácticos, no representaciones globales. [/38] The ambition to map the entire world emerged slowly, as trade, exploration and military conquest created the need to understand distant territories. || Greek scholars made enormous advances in geographical theory. Eratosthenes, working in Alexandria in the third century BC, calculated the circumference of the Earth with remarkable accuracy using only shadows and geometry. (39) [39] Los griegos establecieron las bases científicas de la cartografía. [/39] His successors used these calculations to build coordinate systems of latitude and longitude that, with refinements, remain the basis of modern mapping. || The great age of European exploration in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries produced an explosion of new geographical knowledge — and enormous cartographic confusion. Explorers returned with contradictory observations, coastlines were estimated from incomplete surveys, and unknown continents were sometimes given imaginative shapes. (40) [40] Los primeros mapas del mundo nuevo estaban llenos de errores. [/40] Yet even flawed maps served a purpose: they organised the available information and provided a framework for subsequent, more accurate surveys. || The invention of satellite technology transformed mapmaking in the twentieth century. Remote sensing, GPS and digital imaging have made it possible to map the entire surface of the Earth in extraordinary detail. (41) [41] La tecnología satelital revolucionó la precisión cartográfica. [/41] Yet the fundamental challenges of cartography — representing a three-dimensional sphere on a two-dimensional surface — remain unsolvable; every flat map distorts area, shape, distance or direction to some degree. || Today, maps are everywhere. Digital navigation systems guide billions of journeys daily, and platforms like Google Maps have made personal cartography available to anyone with a smartphone. (42) [42] La cartografía digital ha democratizado el acceso a los mapas. [/42] Yet experts argue that the passive consumption of navigation apps may be eroding our innate sense of spatial awareness — a skill that took our ancestors millennia to develop.

paragraphs.A: They were records of ownership and resource management, not attempts to describe the world in its entirety.
paragraphs.B: Ptolemy's second-century atlas, which codified these advances, was consulted by navigators for over a thousand years.
paragraphs.C: Blank spaces on early maps were often filled with decorative sea monsters or optimistic guesses about what lay beyond the horizon.
paragraphs.D: For the first time, humans could observe the planet from outside it, removing much of the guesswork that had plagued earlier surveys.
paragraphs.E: Online mapping tools and open-source data have also enabled citizen mapping projects that give communities a voice in representing their own territories.
paragraphs.F: A map drawn by a medieval European scholar places Jerusalem at its centre; one drawn in China of the same era naturally centres on China.
paragraphs.G: The speed and detail of modern surveys would have been unimaginable to the sailors who once navigated by hand-drawn charts.

questions.explanation: F concretely illustrates how maps reflect the worldview of their creators, using medieval examples.
A clarifies that the first maps were practical and local documents, not global ones, confirming the description of the Mesopotamian tablets.
B presents Ptolemy's atlas as a direct continuation of Greek scientific advances, including its influence on later navigators.
C describes the creative errors of the first maps of the new world (sea monsters, guesswork) that characterise the cartographic confusion.
D explains the impact of satellites that allow the planet to be observed from outside, eliminating guesswork from earlier surveys.
E adds the democratic dimension of digital cartography with citizen projects, broadening the access mentioned in the paragraph.
